products.title: Bolbitis Heteroclita Difformis
products.body_html:
Bolbitis Difformis "Mini Bolbitis"

Bolbitis Heteroclita Difformis is a truly beautiful, slow growing plant. It grows similar to Anubias and Java Fern. It likes to be tied to rocks or driftwood. Propagation is easy just cut the rhizome after you have a portion you want to move. I recommend only propagating after it has 3 leaves on both sides.

Scientific Name: Bolbitis Heteroclita Difformis

Sold As: Potted / Loose

Care Level: Easy

Lighting Requirement: Low

Co2: Not Needed

Growth Rate: Slow

Origin: Africa

Tank Placement: Midground-Background

products.title: Blue BOA
products.body_html:
Blue BOA Caridina 

Blue BOA Caridina shrimp are a high grade pinto shrimp pattern with distinct cheek spots and full back cape. True BOAs will show a cape that runs cleanly across the entire back line along with visible cheek spots, giving them that bold look. Each shrimp will vary slightly in pattern, which is part of what makes BOAs so fun!

Our Blue BOA Caridina are carefully selected, and the BOAs produced from this line can throw a range of beautiful offspring. Offspring from BOA's can sometimes show up as Blue Steel or Black Galaxy Fishbone Pintos. No matter what these shrimp produce, you'll have amazing colors and patterns! 

Please note that blue coloration is not guaranteed and often does not fully develop until adulthood. Juvies may appear lighter or less defined, with color and pattern improving as they mature

Tank Parameters

  • pH: 5.5-6.7
  • GH: 3-6
  • KH:  0-1
  • TDS: 90-120
  • Temp: 68 to 72°F

Tank Equipment

  • Filter: Matten Filter
  • Active Substrate: Brightwell Rio Escuro or Cafe Soil, Fluval Shrimp Stratum
  • Water: RO/DI Water remineralized with Shrimple GH

products.title: 10 Assorted Galaxy BOA
products.body_html:
Galaxy BOA Assorted Shrimp Pack

Galaxy BOA Assorted Caridina shrimp feature a mix of high-grade BOA patterns with galaxy style spotting. BOA shrimp are a specific pinto pattern recognized by their cheek spots and back cape, and this assortment includes a variety of pattern expressions and coverage levels. Each shrimp is unique, and pattern, color intensity, and galaxy spotting will vary.

This listing is ideal for hobbyists and breeders who enjoy working with BOA genetics without focusing on an exact grade or look. Some shrimp may show stronger capes and heavier galaxy spotting, while others display lighter or developing patterns. As with most Caridina, color and pattern often continue to improve with age, stable parameters, and proper care.

You will receive ten (10) randomly selected shrimp based on what we have available. The shrimp will range in size from juvenile to adult (.25 in to 1 in). These shrimp are hobbyist bred.

Tank Equipment

products.title: Mystery Moss
products.body_html:
Mystery Moss | Aquarium Moss

Mystery Moss is exactly what it sounds like, a fun surprise for planted tank lovers! You’ll receive a healthy portion of one of our aquarium moss varieties, but the exact type will be a mystery. This is a great option if you enjoy trying new plants or just want to add some texture and natural growth to your tank without overthinking it!

Aquarium mosses are incredibly versatile and easy to work with. They can be attached to driftwood, rocks, or decor, left free growing, or used to create dense mats and hiding areas. Moss is especially popular in shrimp tanks, where it provides grazing space, cover for shrimplets, and a natural look that shrimp love.

Mystery Moss is beginner friendly, low maintenance, and does well in a wide range of setups. Growth speed, texture, and appearance will vary depending on the moss you receive, but all varieties we send are suitable for freshwater aquariums and carefully selected before shipping.

This is a fun, affordable way to add variety to your planted tank or shrimp setup, and a great excuse to try a moss you might not normally pick!

 

*All Plants may contain snails or snail eggs. We keep them in all of our tanks for the benefits they provide.*

products.title: Calcium Liquid Test Kit
products.body_html:
Calcium Liquid Test Kit | Fritz Aquatics

Calcium plays a crucial role in aquariums, and it should remain in proper balance with alkalinity (KH) When the balance is off, it can lead to poor growth and tissue necrosis. Calcium uptake in aquariums can change over time, so regular testing is recommended to ensure proper dosing.

While calcium is more closely monitored in saltwater systems, freshwater aquariums also benefit from routine calcium testing. Aquarium plants require calcium for healthy leaf growth, and invertebrates such as shrimp and snails rely on adequate calcium levels to maintain shell strength and support proper molting.

Recommended calcium levels:

  • Tropical community fish: 75+ ppm
  • African cichlids: 120+ ppm
  • Marine aquariums: 300–400 ppm
  • Reef aquariums: 400–450 ppm

Directions & Dosage

  1. Fill the test tube with 5 ml of aquarium water (to the line on the tube).
  2. Add 10 drops of Calcium Test Solution #1, holding the bottle completely vertical for consistent drop size.
  3. Cap the test tube and gently shake to mix. Always use the cap and avoid skin contact, as this may affect test results.
  4. Vigorously shake Calcium Test Solution #2 for at least 10 seconds.
  5. Add Solution #2 drop by drop, gently shaking between drops and counting each one.
  6. Stop when the solution changes from pink to blue. As the endpoint approaches, the color may briefly turn purple; after purple forms, only 1–2 additional drops should be needed.
  7. Calculate calcium level by multiplying the number of drops by 20. Use the chart provided to confirm results.
  8. To lower calcium levels, perform partial water changes using water with lower calcium content.

products.title: Methylene Blue
products.body_html:
Methylene Blue | Aquarium Treatment

Methylene Blue is a commonly used aquarium treatment that helps protect fish, fish eggs, reduce fungal growth, and support fish during stressful situations. It’s often used in breeding setups, quarantine tanks, and hospital tanks where extra support is needed.

This treatment is especially popular with breeders, as it can help prevent fungus on fish eggs and improve hatch rates when used correctly. Methylene Blue can also be used in quarantine or hospital tanks to help reduce stress and support fish recovery. It is not recommended for use in established display tanks, as it can stain silicone, decorations, and negatively impact beneficial bacteria.

Common Uses for Methylene Blue

  • Helps prevent fungus on fish eggs
  • Used in breeding setups to improve hatch rates
  • Supports fish in quarantine or hospital tanks
  • Useful during transport or stress recovery
  • Treats external fungal issues when used properly

Dosing Instructions

  • Standard Treatment:
    Use 10 drops per gallon or 1 teaspoon per 10 gallons to achieve approximately 3 ppm Methylene Blue.
  • During Treatment:
    Remove chemical filtration such as carbon while treating.
    After treatment is complete, replace carbon if needed to help remove the medication.
  • Dip Use:
    Methylene Blue can also be used as a short dip.
    Mix 5 teaspoons per 3 gallons of water.
    Dip fish for no more than 10 seconds, then return them to clean, conditioned water.

Methylene Blue is best used in quarantine, hospital, or breeding setups and is not recommended for display tanks due to staining and its effect on beneficial bacteria.

The Black Banded Leporinus has elongated, dart-like bodies with alternating black bands over a pale yellow/whitish body, making it really stand out in aquariums!

Black Banded Leporinus are omnivores. In the wild, they feed on plants, fruits, and algae, but they will also accept animal protein. In aquariums, they do well on a varied diet of Nano bits, live Daphnia, and occasional bloodworms. A mixed diet helps keep them healthy and reduces the risk of deficiencies.

products.title: 3 White Wizard Snail | Filopaludina martensi
products.body_html:
White Wizard Snail

White Wizard Snails, scientifically known as filopaludina martensi are a fairly uncommon freshwater snail in the hobby, but they’re quickly becoming a favorite! They have bright white shells with a darker tip at the end of it, making them stand out in many tanks!

What makes this snail truly unique is that it can filter feed using its gills, something you don’t see in most aquarium snails! It also grazes on algae, leftover food, and biofilm. White Wizard Snails are livebearers, meaning they give birth to fully developed babies instead of laying eggs. Which makes breeding slow and manageable, so you won’t end up with an overrun tank.

These snails prefer warmer water and do best in stable, established aquariums. They grow larger than many common snails and do well in community tanks with peaceful fish and shrimp.

Please note: During shipping, snails sometimes go into a hibernation-like state and may not become fully active for up to a week once acclimated to your aquarium. 

Tank Parameters

  • pH: 7.2 to 8.5
  • GH: 7 to 15
  • KH:  2 to 8
  • TDS: 120 to 400
  • Temp: 68 to 78

products.title: 3 Black Devil Spike Snail | Faunus Ater
products.body_html:
Black Devil Snail

Black Devil Spike Snails are extremely hardy freshwater snails. Also sometimes referred to as Lava Snails, they're great at algae and substrate cleaning! These are seriously an underrated option if you’re looking for a larger, more active snail.

They have long spike shaped shells that range from rich chocolate brown to a deep, glossy black. When fully grown, they typically reach 2-3 inches, so they do best in tanks with some open space rather than heavily planted or overcrowded setups.

Black Devil Spike Snails naturally come from brackish waters in parts of Asia, including Thailand, but they adapt very well to freshwater aquariums. While they can live long term in freshwater, they will only breed in brackish water, so population control is not a concern.

Compared to Giant Sulawesi Snails, these tend to be more active and move around the tank more. They are peaceful, shrimp safe, and do well with other non-aggressive tankmates! While they primarily graze on algae and detritus, they may occasionally nibble on softer live plants.

Please note: During shipping, snails sometimes go into a hibernation-like state and may not become fully active for up to a week once acclimated to your aquarium. 

Tank Parameters

  • pH: 7.2 to 8.5
  • GH: 7 to 15
  • KH:  2 to 8
  • TDS: 120 to 400
  • Temp: 68 to 78

products.title: Sailfin Molly | Poecilia Velifera
products.body_html:
Sailfin Molly Fish

The Sailfin Molly, also called the Yucatan Molly or Giant Sailfin Molly, is best known for the male’s tall, flowing dorsal fin that looks like a sail! When fully grown, they can reach around 4 inches, making them one of the larger molly types. Despite their size, Sailfin Mollies are peaceful and outgoing, making them a great fit for mixed community aquariums. They do best when kept in groups as they're social fish.

Like all mollies, Sailfin Mollies are livebearers, meaning females give birth to live fry instead of laying eggs. Watching a group of these fish swim together, with males showing off their large dorsal fins, adds plenty of movement and interest to a tank. Hardy and adaptable, they are a solid choice for both new and experienced hobbyists!

We give each Molly at least two weeks of expert care before shipping, then pack them in breather bags inside recycled‑foam boxes.

Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

We feed our fish a mix of frozen, live, and prepared foods including daphnia, baby brine and cyclops. We also supplement their diet with a mix of Northfin Veggie and Community. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • GH: 8 to 16
  • KH:  4 to 7

products.title: Cryptocoryne Wendtii 'Tropica'
products.body_html:
Wendtii Tropica

Wendtii Tropic is a beautiful aquarium plant with dark, hammered leaves, and is named after 'Tropica' When grown in an open space the leaves will almost lie on the bottom. It's suitable for nano aquariums with leaves from 10-20 cm, and a rosette from 10-20 cm wide. Like most other Sri Lankan cryptocorynes, it also grows well in hard water.

Scientific Name: Cryptocoryne Wendtii 'Tropica'
Common Names: Crypt Wendtii Tropica, Crypt Tropica, Water Trumpet

Caring for Cryptocoryne Wendtii 'Tropica'

Care Level:  Easy

Light: Low-Medium

CO2: Not necessary but is recommended

Fertilizer or Shrimp Safe Fertilizer

Propagation: Separate by rhizome

Note: Please do additional research on your plant to ensure its health.

products.title: Red Wag Swordtail (Hi Fin) | Xiphophorus helleri
products.body_html:
Velvet Wag Swordtail | Hi Fin Variation

The Red Wagtail Hi-Fin Swordtail is a hardy and active livebearer. These swordtails have the signature red wag coloration (red body with black tail) along with their swordtail. The unique characteristic is their high fin. These Red Wag swordtails have a long flowing top fin which si very unique and hard to breed for.

Our rag wag high-fin swordtails grow to about 4-6 inches in size. The males are known for their long, black swordtail and tall dorsal fin, which makes them Hi-Fins. While the females have a rounder shape, and lack the distinctive swordtail fins. They show their best colors in a heavily planted aquariums.

Though their not true schooling fish, they are naturally social and do best when kept in groups! To reduce stress and encourage natural behavior, it’s best to keep more females than males, as males can become territorial when competing for mates. We hold each swordtail for at least two weeks before shipping, giving them expert care so they’re healthy and ready for your tank.

Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

Red Lyretails are a livebearer which means they give birth to live fry instead of laying eggs. This makes them easy to breed as long as they are kept in groups of four or more, with a ratio of three females to every male.

products.body_html:
White Mickey Mouse Platy with Orange Tail

The White Mickey Mouse Platy is a hardy and peaceful livebearer that makes a great choice for beginners. They grow to about 2-2.5 inches and are best kept in groups so that they're more likely to display their  natural social behavior. They have bright white bodies with hints of orange and the classic “Mickey Mouse” tail pattern! We hold each of our platies for at least two weeks before shipping, giving them expert care so they are healthy and ready for your aquarium!

Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2-8.2
  • Gh: 8-12
  • Kh: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80

products.title: Blue Mickey Mouse Platy | Xiphophorus maculatus
products.body_html:
Blue Mickey Mouse Platy Fish

The Blue Mickey Mouse Platy is a hardy, peaceful livebearer that makes a great choice for beginners. They grow to about 2-2.5 inches and are best kept in groups where they can show their natural social behavior. With their light blue bodies and the classic “Mickey Mouse” pattern in a deeper blue/black near the tail, they're sure to be a fan favorite! 

Setting the Standard

We hold each of our platies for at least two weeks before shipping, giving them expert care so they are healthy and ready for your aquarium. To reduce stress during shipping, every order is packed in breather bags with recycled insulation and shipped overnight. We back every shipment with our 100% live arrival guarantee and are here to help before, during, and after your order. As hobbyists ourselves, we want to see you succeed and enjoy your new fish.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2-8.2
  • Gh: 8-12
  • Kh: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

products.title: Black Lyretail Molly | Poecilia sp.
products.body_html:
Black Lyretail Molly | Male & Female Mix

The Black Lyretail Molly is a peaceful livebearer that grows to about 4-5 inches long. They have solid black bodies with lyre shaped tail fins that give them their name. Some show a yellow/orange hue in their fins, which stands out against the dark body, we've be saying it looks similar to how lava looks as it dries out! They’re hardy fish that adapt well to different aquariums and get along with most other peaceful fish.

Like all mollies, the Black Lyretail gives birth to live fry instead of laying eggs. They’re best kept in groups with more females than males to reduce stress and keep the group calm. Easy to care for and full of activity, they make a great choice for hobbyists who want a hardy livebearer with unique color and personality.

Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

Our Tank Parameters

  • pH: 7.0 to 8.0
  • GH: 6 to 8
  • KH:  3 to 5
  • TDS: 180 to 250
  • Temp: 72 to 80

products.title: Black Metal Guppy | Poecilia Reticulatus
products.body_html:
Black Metal Guppy

The Black Metal Guppy is a freshwater guppy known for its solid black coloration with it's middle section showing the 'metal' pattern. This variety of Poecilia reticulata is popular in home aquariums for its simple yet striking appearance. The Black Metal Guppy is peaceful and does well in community tanks with similarly calm tankmates.

Black Metal Guppies thrive in water with a pH above 7.0 and a temperature range of 72°F to 82°F. They are omnivorous and require a diet that includes high-quality flakes, pellets, and occasional live or frozen foods. Easy to care for, Black Metal Guppy fish are suitable for hobbyists at all experience levels, adding a unique look to aquarium setups.

Our Tank Parameters:

  • pH: 7.2-8.2
  • GH: 8-12
  • KH: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

products.title: Cuckoo Catfish | Synodontis Petricola
products.body_html:
Syno Petricola | Synodontis Catfish

Cuckoo Catfish, scientifically known as Synodontis Petricola, is a small catfish from Lake Tanganyika in Africa. They grow to about 4-4.5 inches in size. They have cool, leopard like black spots across their body with fins edged in white. These active fish are known for their playful behavior, like swimming upside down, and can even make squeaking or croaking sounds when out of water.

Syno Petricola are peaceful and best kept in groups of at least 4, though larger groups will keep them happier and more social. We hold each catfish for at least two weeks before shipping, giving them expert care so they are healthy and ready for your tank! Our Synodontis catfish are shipped in breather bags and packed into boxes with recycled insulation and sent overnight to reduce stress. 

Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

Synodontis Catfish Food

Synodontis Catfish are mostly carnivorous and do best with a variety of meaty foods. They readily accept frozen or live options like bloodworms, brine shrimp, or daphnia. High-quality sinking pellets or wafers can be offered as a staple. A mixed diet keeps them active, healthy, and showing their best behavior.

Our Tank Parameters for Synodontis

  • pH: 7.2 to 7.5
  • GH: 8 to 16
  • KH: 4 to 7
  • TDS: 180 to 250
  • Temp: 70 to 74

products.title: Odessa Barb | Puntius padamya
products.body_html:
Odessa Barbs | Barb Fish for Sale

Odessa Barbs are one of the most colorful barbs in the aquarium hobby. Males develop bright red and black stripes as they mature, while females stay more silver and plain. The fish we sell are young adults, usually around 6 months old, so their full colors will show as they reach 10–12 months of age.

Barbs are known for being active and sometimes nippy. Odessa Barbs are less aggressive than Tiger Barbs, but we don’t recommend keeping them with very small fish. They are strong eaters and will quickly go after most foods. At the size we sell (about 2–3 inches), they are already larger than many of the fish we carry.

We feed them a mix of NorthFin foods, with Nano Bits as a favorite. They also enjoy frozen foods, and we occasionally give them live daphnia as a treat and to support digestion.

Our Tank Parameters

  • pH: 7-7.8
  • Gh: 5-7
  • Kh: 2-4
  • TDS: 100-150
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: Every fish order is covered by our 100% Live Arrival Guarantee, so you can shop with confidence.

products.title: Norman Lampeye Killifish | Poropanchax normani
products.body_html:
Normans Lamp Eye Killifish

The Norman’s Lampeye Killifish is small, peaceful, and great for beginners due to their easy care. Norman’s Lampeye gets its name from the glowing blue “lamp” above each eye. This nano fish comes from slow waters in Central and West Africa. It swims near the surface, so a tight lid is a must to avoid jumping. If they feel safe, they will display their best color in a planted aquarium with floating plants and a dark background. To ensure they feel safe, it is best to keep the lampeye killifish in groups of 6 or more.

Breeding Lampeye Killifish 

Norman’s Lampeye are easy to breed egg scatterers. They spawn near the surface in floating plants or a yarn spawning mop, laying a few sticky eggs each day. For best results, move the mop or the Lampeye killifish so the eggs and fry aren’t eaten. Eggs usually hatch in about 10 to 14 days. Fry can easily feed off biofilm or naturally occurring infusoria until they are ready to eat live baby daphnia. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • TDS: 160 to 180
  • Temp: 70 to 74°F

products.title: Empire Gudgeon | Hypseleotris compressa
products.body_html:
Gudgeon Empire Fish

The Empire Gudgeon (Hypseleotris compressa) is a peaceful fish from Australia, south-central New Guinea, and parts of Indonesia. It grows to about 4–5 inches. Males show neon reds, blues, and greens and develop a rounded head, especially when breeding. Females stay more muted in color.

Empire gudgeon fish are best kept in small groups of three or more in a planted tank with rocks, driftwood, and hiding spots. They can jump, so use a tight lid or lower the water level to keep them safe. Empire Gudgeons are carnivores that do best on frozen foods and live daphnia. They will likely eat baby shrimp or fry, but they’re usually fine with larger, peaceful community fish.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • TDS: 160 to 180
  • Temp: 70 to 74°F

products.title: Sunset Thicklip Gourami | Colisa labiosa
products.body_html:
Sunset Gourami | Thick Lipped Gourami

The Sunset Thick Lipped Gourami (Colisa labiosa) is a peaceful, hardy fish from southern Myanmar. Like other gouramis, it has a labyrinth organ that lets it breathe air at the surface. It stays relatively small, reaching about 3.5 to 4 inches as an adult. This fish gets its “sunset” name from its golden-orange body and yellow fin edges that resemble a sunset.

The Sunset Thicklip Gourami is generally peaceful. Adult males may show some territorial behavior toward other male gouramis, but they usually coexist in a roomy, planted tank. They rarely show aggression toward other tankmates, which makes them a good fit for community aquariums. They prefer slow-moving water, so a sponge filter or matten filter works well.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • TDS: 160 to 180
  • Temp: 70 to 74°F

products.title: Adolfoi Cory | Corydoras adolfoi
products.body_html:
Corydoras adolfoi | Adolfo's Cory

Adolfo’s Cory (Corydoras adolfoi) is a rare cory from Peru and Brazil. As it matures, it develops a bright yellow-orange head patch with a clean black mask. It stays small and peaceful, so it fits well in planted community aquariums.

Keep them in groups of six or more so they feel safe and show natural behavior. Good tankmates include dwarf cichlids, angelfish, larger shrimp, and other calm fish. They spend most of their time on the bottom scavenging, so use soft sand or smooth gravel to protect their barbels and belly. They may eat baby shrimp.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • TDS: 160 to 180
  • Temp: 70 to 74°F

products.title: Cory Rabauti | Corydoras rabauti
products.body_html:
Rabauti Cory Catfish | Rust Cory

The Rust Cory Catfish (Corydoras rabauti) is a small, peaceful scavenger from the Amazon basin. It is found in parts of Brazil, Peru, and possibly Colombia. Adults reach about 2 to 2.5 inches. As they mature, they show a mix of colors: rusty orange, soft violet, and a hint of metallic green. Similar to other Corydoras species, Rabauti Cories spend most of their time on the bottom, calmly searching for leftover food.

Rabauti Corydoras prefer to be kept in groups of six or more, this could include other cory species if in a community aquarium. They’re great for community tanks and usually do fine with freshwater shrimp, though they may eat tiny shrimp fry. Use soft sand or smooth gravel to protect their barbels and belly. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • TDS: 160 to 180
  • Temp: 70 to 74°F

products.title: Green Swordtail | Xiphophorus helleri
products.body_html:
Green Swordtail Fish

The Green Swordtail (Xiphophorus helleri) is a peaceful livebearer from the rivers and streams of Mexico and Central America. Adults reach about 5 to 6 inches. Males grow a long “sword” on the lower tail fin. They’re active swimmers and do best in larger tanks with open space to to swim.

We recommend keeping Green Swordtails in groups, with two females to each males to reduce stress. Swordtails are livebearers so they will readily breed with little effort. They can also be kept successfully in male only tanks without issue. They’re great community fish for planted aquariums while plant will also add cover for babies if you plan to breed them. Note that they may eat very small shrimp or their own fry, so choose decor and tankmates with that in mind.

products.title: Rosy Loach | Petruichthys sp.
products.body_html:
Rosy Loaches | Nemacheilus sp.

Rosy Loaches (Petruichthys sp. “Rosy”) are tiny, active fish that reach only 1–1.25 inches. They show bright red to orange hues on a slim, classic loach body and can look a bit like Cherry Barbs in color and shape. In our experience, they’re peaceful but bold. The Rosy Loach thrives for us in large species only tanks of 50 or more. If you’re planning to keep this loach, aim for groups of at least four; singles or pairs can become withdrawn since they’re so social.

Rosy Loach Diet

We feed Rosy Loaches NorthFin Flake Foods, especially Kelp Flake, and small pellets. We grind 3 to 4 different NorthFin pellet types into tiny pieces to fit their very small mouths. As a treat, we offer frozen cyclops and daphnia. Small meals once or twice a day work great.

Water Parameters for Rosy Loaches

We keep Rosy Loaches in a pH 7.2–7.6, GH 4–8, KH 2–4, and 70–74°F. They’ve been very hardy for us and adjust well to slightly harder water. We mix tap with RO water then add Shrimpy GH+ to dial things in. Our final TDS is around 180. Keeping their aquarium clean with steady parameters is the most essential step to ensure Rosy Loaches thrive.

Our Tank Parameters:

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • Temp: 70 to 74

products.title: Thuttinjet Stone Loach | Nemacheilus stigmofasciatus
products.body_html:
Stone Loach "Thuttinjet" | Nemacheilus sp.

The Thuttinjet Stone Loach (Nemacheilus stigmofasciatus) is rare and was only recently introduced to the hobby. Even with that, it’s been easy for us to keep and it accepts most foods. This loach is marked by a thin black line that runs down its side like a dotted roadway. It has a mottled pattern similar to a Yo-Yo Loach, with small “Y” and “O” shapes. The face is rounded, a bit like a Siamese Algae Eater.

Since receiving them, we’ve found they’re active and social. For this reason, we recommend keeping a small group of three or more so they have company. As with most loaches, a bit of flow in the tank helps them feel more natural, although it isn’t necessary.

What do Stone Loaches Eat

Nemacheilus stigmofasciatus has been surprisingly easy to feed. We mainly use NorthFin Kelp Flakes, a mix of small pellets (we grind 3–4 NorthFin pellet types), and frozen foods like cyclops and daphnia. Based on their appetite, the Thuttinjet Stone Loach should accept most foods, but keep their small mouths in mind.

Best Parameters for Nemacheilus species

We’ve kept them in semi-hard water with TDS about 180, GH 6–8, KH 2–4, and pH around 7.2–7.8. A setup with soft sand or smooth gravel, good oxygen, and a few hiding spots works well. Although, we keep them in a bare bottom aquarium with a sponge or matten filter with no issues.  With that, these rare loaches settle in quickly and show off their unique pattern.

Our Tank Parameters:

  • Ph: 7.2 to 7.6
  • Gh: 4 to 8
  • Kh:  2 to 4
  • Temp: 70 to 74

products.title: Blue Cobra Guppy | Poecilia Reticulatus
products.body_html:
Blue Cobra Guppy

The Blue Cobra Guppy, a variety of Poecilia reticulata, showcases a unique snakeskin-like pattern across its body, reminiscent of a cobra's scales. This distinctive design is complemented by a vibrant mix of colors, predominantly shades of blue with hints of red and yellow. Its flowing tail adds to its visual appeal, making it a standout in any freshwater aquarium.

Selective breeding has enhanced the Blue Cobra Guppy's intricate patterns and bold colors, resulting in a fish that thrives in community tanks. Their peaceful temperament further contributes to their popularity among aquarists.

Our Tank Parameters:

  • Ph: 7.2-8.2
  • Gh: 8-12
  • Kh: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80

products.title: Neocaridina Shrimp Sale | Buy 5, Get 5
products.body_html:
Buy 5 Neocaridina Shrimp, Get 5 for Free Sale

Our buy 5, get 5 free Neocaridina shrimp offer runs for a very limited time. You pick one variety and we’ll double your order with the same type. All prices are based on a 5 pack of shrimp. You can’t mix varieties, so you’ll get ten of the exact shrimp you select.

Every order also comes with one free gift: alder cones, Indian almond leaves, a Northfin food sampler (3–4 types), or guava leaves. This deal is mainly to celebrate our birthday each July although we may turn this on and off throughout the year, so keep an eye on our site to catch it when it’s live.

Our Tank Parameters for these shrimp

  • Ph: 7.0 to 7.6
  • Gh: 7 to 15
  • Kh:  2 to 8
  • TDS: 150 to 350
  • Temp: 68°F to 72°F

What we feed our shrimp 

  • Northfin Veggie
  • Northfin Community
  • Northfin bug pro
  • Kelp Wafers

Tank Recommendations for these shrimp

  • Minimum tank size: 4 gallons, but 20 gallons recommended
  • Filter: Matten Filter
  • Substrate: Pool Filter Sand, Inert Substrate, or any substrate that will not alter pH

products.title: Bulk Aquatic Plants | Potted Plants
products.body_html:
Bulk Aquarium Plants | Aquatic Potted Plants Only

Looking for a wholesale deal on aquarium plants? Our Bulk Potted Aquatic Plants give you healthy, medium-sized plants at unbeatable prices. Choose the package that fits your tank and budget:

  • 10 plants at $6 each (total $59.99)
  • 25 plants at $5 each (total $124.99)
  • 50 plants at $4 each (total $199.99)

Each plant comes potted in a 2″ plastic pot with rock wool and is ready to go in your tank. You’ll get a random, diverse mix of popular species, think sword plants, cryptocorynes, stem plants and more. This aquarium plants bulk option saves you money and gives you plenty of greenery to fill out your setup.

What to expect from our potted Aquarium Plants:

  • Medium-sized, fully aquatic plants (1½″-14″ tall)
  • Random assortment of species, multiples of the same type may appear
  • Pots clearly labeled for easy planting
  • Feel free to add notes at checkout (low-light plants, background plants, extra variety, etc.), we’ll do our best to match your request

Because we hand-pick each bulk box to give you the best mix, processing may take a bit longer than our usual 5 business days. This ensures you receive a great assortment of plants every time.

Please note: Our bulk aquatic plants may occasionally include hitchhiker snails or duckweed. While we do our best to remove them, a few may slip through.

All in One Aquarium Plant Fertilizer

Keep your new plants healthy and growing strong with our All in One Aquarium Plant Fertilizer | Shrimple. This easy-to-use formula provides everything your plants need:

  • Essential macro and micronutrients
  • Supports lush leaf and root growth
  • Safe for freshwater fish
  • Simple weekly dosing

Just add the recommended dose each week to keep your plants green and vibrant.

Grab your bulk aquarium plants now and turn your tank into a lush, green oasis!

products.title: Bucephalandra Assorted Types
products.body_html:
Assorted Bucephalandra | Potted or Bare Root

Our Assorted Bucephalandra features a variety of low-growing, slow-growing plants native to Borneo. These hardy plants thrive in most aquarium setups with minimal light and fertilization. For the brightest colors and fastest growth, add CO₂ injection, stronger lighting, and good water circulation, but they’ll still do well under basic care.

Plants may arrive potted or with bare roots, depending on availability. Each rhizome will continue to grow new leaves over several months, so be patient, Bucephalandra adds value through its long-lasting, steady growth.

To propagate your Buce, simply cut the rhizome into sections and attach each piece to driftwood or rock with thread or aquarium-safe glue. New leaves will sprout from each section, letting you build a lush carpet or a striking accent over time.

Main Benefits:

  • Low to medium light plant for easy maintenance
  • Easy to attach to decoration in the tank for easy growth
  • Provides hiding places for shrimp and baby shrimp
  • Unique and uncommon plant for your aquarium

More Information: 

You will receive a random piece of Bucephalandra, will usually contain a minimum of 3-4 leaves.

products.title: Bucephalandra 'Godzilla'
products.body_html:
Buce Godzilla | Bucephalandra sp.

Bucephalandra Godzilla is a variation of the plant family Araceae that has a longer leaf with some ruffling, this variation grows a little larger than some species medium in size that is great for a beginning aquarist. The leaves of this variation can turn brown/red in color when grown submerged over a period of time. You can propagate this plant by cutting the rhizome and attaching the piece to driftwood and rocks.

Bucephalandra is usually a low level growing, sometimes flowering plant found in Borneo. It is a undemanding plant in most scenarios, the most vibrant and large growth can be exhibited when using CO2 injection, high light, and good water movement although it can be kept with minimal fertilization and light.

Main Benefits:

  • Low to medium light plant for easy maintenance
  • Easy to attach to decoration in the tank for easy growth
  • Provides hiding places for shrimp and baby shrimp
  • Unique and uncommon plant for your aquarium

More Information: 

You will receive a random piece of Bucephalandra which will usually contain a minimum of 3-4 leaves.

products.title: Bucephalandra Catherinae
products.body_html:
Buce Catherinae | Green Buce

Bucephalandra Catherinae Green is a hardy, slow-growing plant that thrives in low light. You can attach it to driftwood or rocks, where its deep green leaves add rich color and texture. Because it grows slowly, you won’t need to trim it often, and it helps keep your water clean by using extra nutrients. It’s an easy-care choice for beginners and a beautiful accent for any aquarium.

Sold As: Potted Plant / Loose

Care Level: Easy

Lighting Requirement: Low to Medium

Co2: Not Needed but a plus

Growth Rate: Slow

Origin: Borneo

Tank Placement: Foreground - Mid-ground

More About Buce:

Bucephalandra is a relatively new rare plant into the hobby. Don't let that scare you, they are quite easy to grow. They are mostly from South East Asia and are actually in danger of being wiped out due to deforestation on the islands they are found on. Luckily with their popularity gaining in the aquarium hobby they will live on in our tanks. They are truly beautiful plants that all have subtle differences to leaf shape, coloration, and size. They can be grow in both in aquariums and paludraiums. There are hundreds of buce varieties that exist and new ones are being discovered every day. This plant grows similar to anubias as it grows along a rhizome and sends off leaves from it. They do best when tied or glued to rocks or drift wood and should not be planted in the substrate. They are extremely slow growers and take up too 2 years to fully convert in their "submerged" form. As the plant matures in your aquarium the new leaves will become more delicate and have a more intense color to them. To propagate just trim a portion of the rhizome with leaves off and place somewhere.

products.title: Rotala Rotundifolia Orange Juice
products.body_html:
Rotala Orange Juice | Stem Plant

With its distinct orange hue, this plant offers a refreshing twist on its popular relative, Rotala Rotundifolia. Whether submerged or thriving above water, it adapts with grace, showcasing rounder leaves when grown emersed and slender ones when submerged. With a boost of iron and Plant Food fertilizer, its color intensifies, making it a standout amidst the usual greens. Perfect for background placement, it injects subtle contrast into aquascapes.

*Rapid changes in tank parameters can result in the melting of the plant

You will receive one (1) bunch that usually includes 3-5 stems of this plant. Portion size may vary. 


Scientific Name: Rotala rotundifolia "orange"

Light Requirements: High

Care Level: Easy

CO2: Recommended for optimal growth

products.title: Shrimp Stix by Shrimp Envy
products.body_html:
Shrimp Stix

Shrimp Stix are a wholesome treat you can use when you want to lure your shrimp out of all their hiding spots for photos or if you just want to watch them for your own enjoyment! These sticks are made with a mix of leaves and beneficial algae such as Spirulina, mulberry, stinging nettle, dandelion, moringa, kale, spinach, chlorella, bee pollen, kelp, alfalfa, wheatgrass, pea protein, aloe vera, black walnut, beetroot, montmorillonite, and blackberry. These are sure to be a favorite, and even better, they provide whole tank complete nutrition for your shrimp and the babies!

Instructions: Turn your water movement down. Simply stick the shrimp stix into your substrate or cholla. Enjoy the show! Stix and Lil Stix are for occasional use only, overuse can lead to nitrate issues without proper water changes.

8 inch Shrimp Stix will last 45 to 60 minutes or longer in your tank. 4 inch Lil' Stix will last roughly 20-40 minutes,The shrimp will aggressively eat it clean and release some of the powder to disperse around your tank for the babies. Also please be mindful, warm tanks with medium/high flow will make stix not last as long. Put the stick in the area of least flow in the tank for best results.

products.title: Monti-Clay by Shrimp Envy
products.body_html:
Monti-Clay

Montmorillonite clay is a valuable resource as it helps to stabilize pH but also helps to control nuisance algae. While helping to keep your water crystal clear, it performs much better than activated charcoal as it binds heavy metal ions and toxins in the water. It also helps to promote the growth of beneficial bacteria which is critical to the ecosystem of your aquarium. Montmorillonite clay also contains essential minerals for healthy shrimp and proper molting.

Shrimp envy Monti-Clay contains 25% Mironekuton mineral powder as well. Mironekuton is a deep-sea mineral clay that contains over 66 minerals and trace elements. We dose this weekly in all of our shrimp tanks at Shrimp Envy.

Your pouch of monti-clay also includes a scoop for easy dosing.

Recommended dosage: 1-2 scoops per week per 10 gallons of water.

About Shrimp Envy: Shrimp Envy offers premium, all-natural plant and mineral-based shrimp foods, meticulously handmade in the United States by fellow shrimp enthusiasts. Shrimp Envy's products contain no fillers, fishmeal, or junk—just pure, wholesome ingredients. products.title: Japan Blue Double Swordtail Guppy
products.body_html:
Japan Blue Guppy Double Swordtail 

The Japan Blue Guppy, a stunning aquatic gem that will bring a touch of the exotic to your freshwater aquarium. Renowned for its vivid blue coloration and striking fin patterns, this unique guppy variant is a true showstopper. Dive into the world of the Japan Blue Guppy with the following details:

Species: Japan Blue Guppy (Poecilia reticulata)

Description: The Japan Blue Guppy is a selectively bred variant of the popular guppy species, known for its distinct and intense blue coloration. These guppies exhibit vibrant hues that can range from electric blue to deep sapphire. Their bodies are adorned with intricate and eye-catching fin patterns, creating a visually stunning contrast against the vivid blue backdrop.

Size: Japan Blue Guppies typically reach a size of around 1 to 2 inches (2.5 to 5 centimeters), making them an ideal choice for aquarium enthusiasts of all experience levels. Their compact size and striking appearance make them a sought-after addition to any tank.

Behavior: These guppies are known for their active and social nature. They enjoy exploring their surroundings and interacting with other fish in the tank. Their peaceful temperament makes them well-suited for community aquariums, where they coexist harmoniously with other non-aggressive fish species.

Water Parameters:

  • Temperature: 75°F to 82°F (24°C to 28°C)
  • pH: 6.5 to 8.0
  • Hardness: 10 to 20 dGH

Care Guidelines:

  • Diet: Japan Blue Guppies are omnivores and thrive on a balanced diet of high-quality flake food, pellets, live or frozen foods like brine shrimp, and daphnia.
  • Tank Size: A 10-gallon tank or larger is suitable for a small group of these guppies.
  • Filtration: Maintain good filtration and conduct regular water changes to ensure optimal water quality.

Breeding: Japan Blue Guppies are prolific breeders, making them an exciting choice for those interested in breeding fish. They give birth to live fry, and providing adequate hiding spots for the fry is crucial to their survival.

Note: When considering Japan Blue Guppies, be sure to maintain appropriate water conditions and check for compatibility with other fish in your aquarium.

Elevate your aquatic display with the captivating allure of the Japan Blue Guppy. Their mesmerizing blue colors and intricate fin patterns are sure to leave a lasting impression on your freshwater aquarium. Don't miss the opportunity to introduce this extraordinary fish into your aquatic world. Order your Japan Blue Guppies today and watch your aquarium come alive with vibrant shades of blue.

 

Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with the Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

What we feed Guppies

We feed our fish a mix of frozen, live, and prepared foods including live baby brine and black worms. We also supplement their diet with a mix of Northfin Veggie, Community, and Bug Pro. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 6.5 to 7.8
  • Gh: 6 to 18
  • Kh:  2 to 6
  • TDS: 100 to 200
  • Temp: 72 to 80

products.title: White Spotted Rabbit Snail
products.body_html:
White Spotted Rabbit Aquarium Snails

White Spotted Rabbit snails resemble Malaysian trumpet snails in shape but rabbit snails are much larger in size. The shape and color of rabbit snails are slightly different as well. They are equally as valuable for your aquarium as Malaysian trumpet snails making them a great alternative. Snails will be equal to or larger than 1/4 of an inch.

Main Benefits:

  • Snails help keep a shrimp tank from re-cycling by provided adequate bio-load
  • By burrowing into the substrates, these snails help keep turn the substrate which prevents gas build up. 
  • Snails eat left over food to prevent ammonia spikes
  • Hardy so they can adjust to most water parameters 

When Receiving Snails: 

  1. Place the snails in a corner of the tank and make a mental note where they were place
  2. Watch the snails over 7 days, if they do not move remove them from the aquarium
  3. Give the snails a smell and if they stink, then they are dead
  4. If they do not smell, add them back to the aquarium and give it another 3 days
  5. It is common for snails to burrow into the substrate and disappear from sight

More Information: 

 Also know as the elephant snails, rabbit snails are very peaceful in nature. They have a great temperament to them and they enjoy exploring the aquarium. They are never aggressive to other inhabitants. Rabbit snails tend to be active throughout the day and night. They do not favor one time or the other to be active. 

Care Parameters: 

  • TDS between 200 and 400 
  • GH between 6 and 15
  • KH between 4 and 8
  • PH around 7.2 to 7.8
  • These parameters are what we keep them at, they can adjust to other parameters as well. Try to stay on the harder side rather than softer water.

products.title: Albino African Dwarf Frog | Hymenochirus Boettgeri
products.body_html:
Albino African Dwarf Frog

The Albino African Dwarf Frog, also known as the Blonde African Dwarf Frog, is a  unique addition to any freshwater aquarium. With its pale, almost golden appearance and webbed feet, this frog is full of personality. Unlike many other frogs, it is fully aquatic and spends its entire life underwater, making it an ideal choice for aquarium enthusiasts.

Albino African Dwarf Frogs do well in community tanks with other non-aggressive fish and shrimp. They’re easy to care for and enjoy a varied diet of frozen or live foods, like bloodworms and brine shrimp, as well as high-quality sinking pellets. These frogs are curious, active swimmers that love exploring their tank. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7-7.8
  • Gh: 5-7
  • Kh: 2-4
  • TDS: 100-150
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with the Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions:

1. How big do Albino African Dwarf Frogs get?

These frogs usually grow to about 2.5 inches in length when fully mature.

2. What do Albino African Dwarf Frogs eat?

They enjoy a diet of frozen or live foods, such as bloodworms and brine shrimp, along with high-quality sinking pellets. Feed them 3-4 times a week.

3. Can they live with fish?

Yes, they are peaceful and can live with non-aggressive fish species. Avoid pairing them with fish that may nip at them or outcompete them for food.

4. Do they need a filter in their tank?

Yes, a filter is recommended to maintain clean water. However, choose a gentle filter to avoid strong currents that may stress the frogs.

5. How much space do they need?

A minimum tank size of 5 to 10 gallons is ideal for a small group of these frogs. Larger tanks provide more space for exploration and stability in water conditions.

6. Do they need a fully aquatic environment?

Yes, Albino African Dwarf Frogs are fully aquatic and do not require access to dry land. Ensure the tank has a secure lid, as they can jump.

7. What kind of tankmates are best for these frogs?

Peaceful tankmates like small tetras, guppies, or snails are great choices. Avoid aggressive fish or fish that might see the frogs as food.

8. Are they good for beginner aquarists?

Yes! Albino African Dwarf Frogs are hardy, low-maintenance, and an excellent choice for beginners looking for something unique.

9. Do they need hiding spots?

Yes, providing hiding spots like plants, caves, or decorations helps them feel secure and mimic their natural environment.

10. How can you tell males from females?

Females are generally larger and have a rounder body, while males are slimmer and may have a small white gland behind their front legs.

products.title: Caridina Babaulti 'Malaya'
products.body_html:
Caridina Babaulti 'Malaya'

Caridina Babaulti is a diverse group of shrimp, but one with very little research. What we do know is that the majority of shrimp within this species come from India, and while they are considered Caridina Shrimp, they are much hardier than a typical Caridina. 

The Caridina cf. babaulti 'Malaya' shrimp is a unique and adaptable species that catches the attention of aquarists with its natural beauty and fascinating behavior. These shrimp are known for their variable coloration, ranging in different earthy tones with subtle stripes or patterns, making each individual a standout addition to a freshwater aquarium. Their natural habitat includes slow-moving, vegetation-rich streams and rivers in Southeast Asia, which influences their preferences for stable water conditions and a planted environment.

products.title: Sunray Guppy | Poecilia Reticulatus
products.body_html:
Sunray Guppy

The Sunray Guppy, a freshwater fish, features a vibrant golden-yellow body with flowing fins. These fish thrive in community tanks, making them a great addition if you have other peaceful livestock. Like other guppies, Sunray Guppies are hardy and adaptable, making them an excellent choice for both beginner and experienced aquarists.

These guppies are livebearers, known for their fascinating breeding behavior, and they prefer aquariums with plenty of plants and hiding spots. Sunray Guppies are omnivorous, enjoying a diet of high-quality flakes, pellets, and occasional treats like frozen or live foods.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2-8.2
  • Gh: 8-12
  • Kh: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with
Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions:

1. How big do Sunray Guppies get?

Sunray Guppies typically reach a size of 1.5 to 2 inches, making them an excellent choice for small to medium-sized aquariums.

2. What tank size is best for Sunray Guppies?

A 5-gallon tank is suitable for a small group, but a 10-gallon or larger aquarium is ideal to give them ample space to swim and thrive.

3. Are Sunray Guppies peaceful fish?

Yes, Sunray Guppies are peaceful and make great additions to community tanks. They get along well with other small, non-aggressive species such as tetras, Corydoras, and rasboras.

4. What should I feed my Sunray Guppies?

As omnivores, they enjoy a varied diet. Offer them high-quality flake food or micro pellets, and supplement with live or frozen treats like brine shrimp, daphnia, or bloodworms to enhance their vibrant coloration.

5. Do Sunray Guppies breed easily?

Yes, Sunray Guppies are livebearers and breed readily in aquariums. If males and females are kept together, you may soon notice baby guppies (fry) in your tank.

6. How can I keep their colors vibrant?

To showcase their radiant sunlit patterns, provide a balanced diet, stable water conditions, and adequate lighting. Regular water changes and a stress-free environment will also help maintain their bright and healthy appearance.

7. Can I keep Sunray Guppies with other guppy varieties?

Yes, they coexist peacefully with other guppy varieties. However, if you're focused on breeding specific traits, it's best to separate them to prevent crossbreeding.

A 5-gallon tank can accommodate a small group, but a 10-gallon or larger tank is recommended to give them ample space for swimming and thriving.

Yes, Pink Tuxedo Guppies coexist peacefully with other guppy varieties. However, if you want to maintain specific traits, consider separating them to avoid crossbreeding.

products.title: Half Black Leopard Guppy
products.body_html:
Half Black Leopard Guppy

The Half Black Leopard guppy or Poecilia reticulata is a true classic in any aquarium, and many hobby aquarists often start out with guppies because of how easy they are to care for, and how easy they are to breed. The Half-Black Leopard Guppy isn't any different in this perspective, and its unique appearance makes it a real sight to see.

For whom is the Half-Black Leopard Guppy suited?

Like most other guppies, the Half-Black Leopard Guppy is easy to care for and is entirely suitable for beginner aquarists. Especially if you'd like to give breeding a try, the guppy is the perfect fish, and having an aquarium filled with little Leopard Guppies will certainly create a unique aesthetic.

What are they like?

The Half-Black Leopard Guppy is a very social fish and can easily co-exist with a large variety of other fish, invertebrates and aquarium snails. As mentioned before, these fish love to procreate, and so if you don't want to have an aquarium filled with these beautiful little fish, we suggest either introducing a natural predator to keep the population in check, or to only hold male guppies.

The ideal conditions for the Half-Black Leopard Guppy

Guppies thrive in planted tanks low to moderate flowing water. As livebearers they do well in mixed sexed groups although they will eat their young if given the chance. Be sure to provide areas of cover for offspring using floating plants, botanicals such as Indian Almond leaves and rock or wood structures. 

Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

What we feed Guppies

Guppies are natural omnivores and should be fed a varied diet through prepared, live, and frozen foods. We feed them with a mix live foods such as baby brine, Daphnia, Bloodworms, Blackworms, scuds, and also Northfin Veggie, Community, and Bug Pro prepared foods to ensure a balanced and nutritious diet.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 6.5 to 7.8
  • Gh: 6 to 18
  • Kh:  2 to 6
  • TDS: 100 to 200
  • Temp: 72 to 80

products.title: Buenos Aires Tetra
products.body_html:
Buenos Aires Tetra

The Buenos Aires Tetra is a freshwater species known for its sleek, metallic silver body that reflects the light in an aquarium setting. Its fins are marked with shades of orange or red, with a distinctive black stripe running through the tail fin. With its silver body and unique red and black markings, the Buenos Aires Tetra will stand out in any aquarium.

Buenos Aires tetras grow up to 2.5 inches and exhibit a streamlined body shape designed for their active swimming habits. They are best kept in groups of six or more to allow their natural schooling behavior to emerge. Their vibrant coloration is most pronounced when housed in well-maintained aquariums with appropriate water conditions and a varied diet that includes flakes, live, or frozen foods.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 5.5-6.9
  • Gh: 3-5
  • Kh: 0-1
  • TDS: 80-100
  • Temp: 72-80

Tank Recommendations

  • Leaves: Indian Almond or Guava
  • Wood: Malaysian
  • Cones: Alder Cones
  • Other: Buffered substrate to lower pH, not required
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions

1. How big do Buenos Aires Tetras get?

These fish typically grow to about 2.5 inches in length, making them a mid-sized option suitable for community aquariums.

2. Do Buenos Aires Tetras need to be kept in groups?

Yes, they are schooling fish and should be kept in groups of at least six. Being in a group helps them feel secure and encourages natural behavior.

3. What should I feed Buenos Aires Tetras?

They are omnivores and should be fed a varied diet, including high-quality flakes, pellets, and frozen or live foods like bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Occasional vegetable matter, such as blanched spinach or zucchini, can also be offered.

4. Are Buenos Aires Tetras aggressive?

While generally peaceful, they are active and may nip at the fins of slower-moving or long-finned tankmates. They do best with similarly robust and active fish.

5. What tank size is recommended for Buenos Aires Tetras?

A minimum tank size of 20 gallons is recommended, with larger tanks being better for groups and providing enough swimming space.

6. How long do Buenos Aires Tetras live?

With proper care, Buenos Aires Tetras can live for about 5 to 6 years in a home aquarium

products.title: Lavender Gourami
products.body_html:
Lavender Gourami

The lavender gourami (Trichopodus trichopterus), a variety of the three-spot gourami, is a peaceful and hardy freshwater fish perfect for beginner and experienced aquarists alike. Known for its soft lavender hue, this fish thrives in community tanks with other calm species. With a mature size of up to 5 inches, the lavender gourami requires an aquarium of at least 20 gallons. Larger setups are preferred when keeping multiple individuals or a mixed-gender group.

Lavender gouramis enjoy densely planted aquascapes with hiding spots like driftwood or rocks, complemented by floating plants to create shaded areas. These unique fish are also known for their ability to breathe air directly. Their calm temperament allows them to coexist with tetras, rasboras, and other non-aggressive species, but it's best to avoid tankmates that nip fins or outcompete for food.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7-7.8
  • Gh: 5-7
  • Kh: 2-4
  • TDS: 100-150
  • Temp: 72-80

Tank Recommendations

  • Leaves: Indian Almond or Guava
  • Wood: Malaysian
  • Cones: Alder Cones
  • Other: Buffered substrate to lower pH, not required
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions:

1. What tank size is best for lavender gouramis?
A single lavender gourami requires a tank of at least 20 gallons. For groups, a larger tank is recommended to ensure enough swimming space and to minimize territorial behavior.

2. Are lavender gouramis peaceful?
Yes, they are generally peaceful but can become territorial, especially males, during breeding or in smaller tanks. They are best kept with other calm fish like tetras, rasboras, and corydoras.

3. What do lavender gouramis eat?
These omnivores enjoy a diet of high-quality pellets or flakes, supplemented with live or frozen foods like brine shrimp, bloodworms, and daphnia. Occasionally offering vegetable-based foods is also beneficial.

4. Can I keep more than one lavender gourami in the same tank?
Yes, but it’s best to keep one male with multiple females or in groups of four or more to minimize aggression. Providing a larger tank with plenty of hiding spots is key for success.

5. How do I differentiate between male and female lavender gouramis?
Males tend to have brighter colors, longer dorsal fins, and a slimmer body shape, while females are rounder and slightly duller in coloration.

6. What are good tankmates for lavender gouramis?
They get along well with other peaceful species like harlequin rasboras, neon tetras, and kuhli loaches. Avoid fin-nipping fish like barbs and aggressive tankmates like cichlids.

7. Are lavender gouramis hardy fish?
Yes, they are resilient and adaptable, making them suitable for beginners. However, they still require stable water parameters and good tank maintenance to thrive.

What we feed Gouramis

We feed our fish a mix of frozen, live and prepared foods including daphnia, baby brine and cyclops. We also supplement their diet with a mix of Northfin Veggie and Community.

products.title: Black Ruby Barb
products.body_html:
Black Ruby Barb

The Black Ruby Barb is a stunning and active fish, known for its red and black coloration. This hardy fish, also known as the Ruby Barb, thrives in schools and brings lively movement to your tank. Their metallic look and active behavior make them a favorite among aquarists.

Black Ruby Barbs are peaceful, but they do best when kept in groups of at least six. They enjoy swimming in the middle and upper levels of the tank. These fish are easy to care for and adapt well to a range of water conditions. A varied diet of quality flakes, pellets, and occasional live or frozen foods will keep them healthy and vibrant. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7-7.8
  • Gh: 5-7
  • Kh: 2-4
  • TDS: 100-150
  • Temp: 72-80

Tank Recommendations

  • Wood: Malaysian

  • Other: Buffered substrate to lower pH, not required
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions:

1. How big do Black Ruby Barbs get?

Black Ruby Barbs typically grow to about 2 to 3 inches in length, making them suitable for both small and medium-sized tanks.

2. What tank size is best for Black Ruby Barbs?

A minimum tank size of 20 gallons is recommended for a small school of Black Ruby Barbs, though larger tanks are ideal to give them more room to swim and thrive.

3. Are Black Ruby Barbs good for beginners?

Yes, Black Ruby Barbs are relatively easy to care for and are a great choice for beginners. They are hardy, adaptable, and can tolerate a range of water conditions.

4. Do Black Ruby Barbs need to be kept in groups?

Yes, Black Ruby Barbs are social fish and do best when kept in schools of at least 6 or more. They are more active and show off their bright colors when they feel comfortable in a group.

5. What do Black Ruby Barbs eat?

Black Ruby Barbs are omnivores and enjoy a varied diet. They will happily eat high-quality flakes, micro pellets, and live or frozen foods like brine shrimp, daphnia, or bloodworms.

6. Can Black Ruby Barbs live with other fish?

Yes, Black Ruby Barbs are peaceful and do well in community tanks with other non-aggressive species. They get along well with small fish like tetras, livebearers, and Corydoras.

7. What makes Black Ruby Barbs unique?

Black Ruby Barbs are unique because of their beautiful red and gold coloring, especially when kept in well-maintained tanks with stable water parameters. They’re active swimmers and are visually striking in a group.

8. Do Black Ruby Barbs breed easily?

Black Ruby Barbs can breed in aquariums under the right conditions. They are egg layers and may spawn in a well-planted tank, but their eggs should be removed quickly to prevent them from being eaten by the adults.

9. How can I enhance the color of my Black Ruby Barbs?

To bring out their vibrant colors, ensure they are fed a nutritious, balanced diet, maintain stable water conditions, and provide plenty of hiding spots and plants in their tank. The right environment will help them feel secure and show off their best hues.

Yes, Coppertail Guppies are peaceful and do well in community tanks. They get along with other small, non-aggressive species such as tetras, Corydoras, and rasboras.

4. What should I feed my Coppertail Guppies?

These guppies are omnivorous and enjoy a varied diet. Offer high-quality flake food or micro pellets, supplemented with live or frozen foods like brine shrimp, daphnia, or bloodworms to enhance their striking colors.

5. Do Coppertail Guppies breed easily?

Yes, Coppertail Guppies are livebearers and breed readily in aquariums. When males and females are kept together, you may quickly find fry appearing in your tank.

6. Can I keep Coppertail Guppies with other guppy varieties?

Yes, Coppertail Guppies coexist peacefully with other guppy varieties. However, if you want to maintain specific traits, consider separating them to avoid crossbreeding.

products.title: Sunset Guppy | Poecilia Reticulatus
products.body_html:
Sunset Guppy

The Sunset Guppy (Poecilia reticulata) is a freshwater fish known for its vibrant coloration. Its body features a warm gradient of oranges, yellows, and reds which is where its name comes from. The males typically display brighter and more intricate patterns compared to females, with colorful tails that enhance their visual appeal.

Sunset Guppies are an excellent option for hobbyists seeking to add color to their tank. Their unique patterns, which are often likened to fingerprints, ensure that no two guppies look exactly alike, further adding to their beauty.

Our Tank Parameters:

  • Ph: 7.2-8.2
  • Gh: 8-12
  • Kh: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80

Frequently Asked Questions:

1. How big do Sunset Guppies get?

Sunset Guppies typically grow to about 1.5 to 2 inches in length, making them ideal for small to medium-sized aquariums.

2. What tank size is best for Sunset Guppies?

A 5-gallon tank can house a small group of guppies, but a 10-gallon or larger tank is recommended to give them ample swimming space and promote their well-being.

3. Are Sunset Guppies peaceful fish?

Yes, Sunset Guppies are peaceful and thrive in community tanks. They coexist well with other small, non-aggressive species such as tetras, Corydoras, and rasboras.

4. What should I feed my Sunset Guppies?

These guppies are omnivorous and enjoy a varied diet. Provide them with high-quality flake food or micro pellets, and supplement their diet with live or frozen treats like brine shrimp, daphnia, or bloodworms to enhance their vibrant colors.

5. Do Sunset Guppies breed easily?

Yes, Sunset Guppies are livebearers and breed readily in aquariums. If you keep males and females together, you may soon notice fry appearing in your tank.

6. How can I keep their colors vibrant?

To maintain their stunning sunset hues, offer a balanced diet, ensure stable water conditions, and provide adequate lighting. Regular water changes and minimizing stress also help bring out their best coloration.

7. Can I keep Sunset Guppies with other guppy varieties?

Yes, Sunset Guppies can peacefully share a tank with other guppy varieties. However, if you want to maintain specific traits, consider separating them to prevent crossbreeding.

products.title: Bleeding Heart Platy | Xiphophorus maculatus
products.body_html:
Bleeding Heart Platy

The Bleeding Heart Platy (Xiphophorus maculatus) is a peaceful livebearer that makes a fantastic addition to freshwater aquariums. With its red coloration this fish is perfect for aquarists looking to add a splash of color. Bleeding Heart Platies are hardy, making them ideal for beginners and seasoned hobbyists alike. They thrive in community setups with other peaceful fish and appreciate a planted environment, though they can adapt to various aquarium setups.

These fish are omnivorous and enjoy a diet of high-quality flakes, pellets, and frozen foods, ensuring they remain healthy and active. A secure aquarium lid is recommended, as they may jump when startled. 

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 7.2-8.2
  • Gh: 8-12
  • Kh: 4-6
  • TDS: 150-200
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions

1. What tank size is best for Bleeding Heart Platies?

A tank of at least 10 gallons is recommended. Larger tanks are better for groups or community setups.

2. Are Bleeding Heart Platies compatible with other fish?

Yes, they are peaceful and do well with non-aggressive fish like guppies, tetras, and corydoras. They can also coexist with peaceful invertebrates, though they might prey on very small shrimp or their fry.

3. What do Bleeding Heart Platies eat?

They are omnivores and thrive on high-quality flakes or pellets supplemented with frozen or live foods like brine shrimp and bloodworms. Occasional vegetable matter or algae-based food is beneficial.

4. Do Bleeding Heart Platies need a planted tank?

Plants are not required but are appreciated for shelter and foraging. Live plants like Java Moss, Anubias, or Hornwort are great additions.

5. Do Bleeding Heart Platies reproduce easily?

Yes, they are prolific livebearers. Females can give birth to up to 50 fry every month under good conditions. Population control may require separating sexes or adding predatory fish in larger tanks.

What we feed Platies:

We feed our fish a mix of frozen, live and prepared foods including live baby brine and black worms. We also supplement their diet with a mix of Northfin Veggie, Community and Bug Pro.

products.title: Golden Kuhli Loach
products.body_html:
Golden Kuhli Loach

The Golden Kuhli Loach, also known as the Pangio dorae, is a unique and friendly fish. With its slender, eel-like body and warm golden color, this loach stands out at the bottom of the tank. Golden Kuhli Loaches are peaceful and get along well with other fish, making them great for community tanks. They love to hide and explore, so providing caves, driftwood, and plants makes them feel at home.

These loaches are active at night and prefer a soft, sandy substrate where they can burrow and search for food. As scavengers, Golden Kuhli Loaches help keep the tank clean by eating leftover food. They thrive on a balanced diet of sinking pellets, algae wafers, and treats like frozen or live foods.

Our Tank Parameters:

  • Ph: 7-7.8
  • Gh: 5-7
  • Kh: 2-4
  • TDS: 100-150
  • Temp: 72-80

Tank Recommendations:

  • Rock: Smooth rock (round in shape)
  • Leaves: Indian Almond or Guava
  • Cones: Alder, Giant Alder, Cyprus or Casuarina 
  • Plants: Java Ferns, Anubias, Sagittaria, Cryptocorene
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions

1. How big do Golden Kuhli Loaches get?

Golden Kuhli Loaches grow to about 3 to 4 inches in length, making them a good fit for a variety of tank sizes.

2. What tank size is best for Golden Kuhli Loaches?

A minimum tank size of 20 gallons is recommended for Golden Kuhli Loaches. Since they enjoy hiding spots and exploring the bottom, a larger tank gives them plenty of room to feel secure.

3. Are Golden Kuhli Loaches peaceful fish?

Yes, Golden Kuhli Loaches are very peaceful fish. They do well in community tanks and get along with other non-aggressive species. Because they are shy, they benefit from tank mates that won’t disturb them.

4. What should I feed Golden Kuhli Loaches?

Golden Kuhli Loaches are omnivores and thrive on a varied diet. Sinking pellets, algae wafers, and live or frozen foods like bloodworms and brine shrimp are all excellent choices. They’ll also scavenge for leftover food, helping keep the tank clean.

5. Do Golden Kuhli Loaches need a specific type of substrate?

Yes, they prefer a soft, sandy substrate where they can burrow safely. Rough or sharp gravel can harm their delicate bodies, so sand or fine gravel is ideal.

6. Do Golden Kuhli Loaches need to be kept in groups?

Yes, Golden Kuhli Loaches are social and feel more comfortable in small groups. Keeping at least three to six together encourages them to be more active and reduces their shyness.

products.title: Black Emperor Tetra | Nematobrycon Palmeri
products.body_html:
Black Emperor Tetra

The Black Emperor Tetra, also called the Emperor Black Tetra or Black Palmeri, is a freshwater fish that adds bold color to your aquarium. The Black Emperor Tetra's dark body with hints of iridescence makes it stand out in any community aquarium. These peaceful fish thrive in groups and are well-suited for community tanks with other small, non-aggressive species.

Black Emperor Tetras are hardy and easy to care for, making them suitable for both beginners and experienced hobbyists. They prefer a planted tank with open swimming areas and enjoy a diet of flakes, small pellets, and occasional treats like brine shrimp or daphnia. Black Emperor Tetras have a calm nature, making them a wonderful addition to freshwater community setups.

Our Tank Parameters

  • Ph: 5.5-6.9
  • Gh: 3-5
  • Kh: 0-1
  • TDS: 80-100
  • Temp: 72-80
Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with
Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions:

1. How big do Black Emperor Tetras get?

Black Emperor Tetras typically grow to about 1.5 to 2 inches in length, making them a great choice for small to medium-sized aquariums.

2. What tank size is best for Black Emperor Tetras?

A minimum tank size of 15 gallons is recommended for a small group of Black Emperor Tetras. Larger tanks are ideal if you want to keep them in a larger school or mix them with other fish.

3. Are Black Emperor Tetras peaceful fish?

Yes, Black Emperor Tetras are peaceful and social. They do well in schools of six or more and make excellent tank mates for other non-aggressive species like rasboras, guppies, or Corydoras.

4. What should I feed Black Emperor Tetras?

Black Emperor Tetras are omnivorous and enjoy a variety of foods. High-quality flakes or micro pellets make up a good base diet, while live or frozen treats like brine shrimp, daphnia, or bloodworms are excellent supplements.

5. Can Black Emperor Tetras live with other fish?

Absolutely! They are excellent community fish and can coexist peacefully with other similarly sized, non-aggressive species. Avoid housing them with large or aggressive fish that may intimidate or prey on them.

6. Do Black Emperor Tetras breed easily?

While they can breed in aquariums, creating the right conditions takes some effort. A separate breeding tank with slightly acidic water and plenty of fine-leaved plants or spawning mops can encourage spawning behavior.

7. What makes Black Emperor Tetras unique?

Their dark, shimmering coloration and graceful swimming style make them stand out in any aquarium. They are also known for their calm temperament and adaptability, making them a favorite among aquarists.

Red Wag Platy fish are hardy and simple to care for, making them suitable for both beginners and experienced aquarists. Red Wag Platies do well in planted tanks with open swimming areas and enjoy a diet of flakes, pellets, and occasional treats like brine shrimp or daphnia. As livebearers, they may even provide the excitement of raising fry. The Red Wag Platy is a lively and enjoyable choice for freshwater setups.

Red Wag Platies, or Xiphophorus maculatus, usually grow to about 2 to 2.5 inches in length. Their small size makes them ideal for community aquariums and various tank sizes.

2. What tank size is best for Red Wag Platies?

A minimum of 10 gallons is recommended for Red Wag Platies, but larger tanks allow for more activity and room for other tank mates. Because they are active swimmers, they enjoy having space to explore.

3. Are Red Wag Platies peaceful fish?

Yes, Red Wag Platies are peaceful and get along well with other community fish. They are friendly and can live happily with other peaceful species of similar size.

4. What should I feed Red Wag Platies?

Red Wag Platies are omnivores and do well on a varied diet. High-quality flake food, small pellets, and occasional treats like brine shrimp, daphnia, or bloodworms keep them healthy and colorful.

5. Do Red Wag Platies breed easily?

Yes, Red Wag Platies are livebearers, meaning they give birth to live young rather than laying eggs. They breed easily in the right conditions, so you may soon find baby platies in your tank if you have both males and females together.

What we feed Platy fish

We feed our fish a mix of frozen, live and prepared foods including live baby brine and black worms. We also supplement their diet with a mix of Northfin Veggie, Community and Bug Pro.

products.title: Serpae Tetra | Hyphessobrycon eques
products.body_html:
Serpae Tetra

The Serpae Tetra is a striking addition to any freshwater aquarium. With its red body and black fin markings, the Serpae Tetra stands out among plants and decor. As schooling fish, Serpae Tetras thrive in groups of six or more, allowing them to interact and show natural behaviors. In groups, their active nature is especially noticeable.  

Serpae Tetras are hardy and easy to care for, making them a good option for aquarists of all levels. They do well in tanks with plants, moderate lighting, and gentle water flow. To keep them healthy and vibrant, feed them a mix of flakes, small pellets, and occasional treats like brine shrimp. These active fish bring energy and color to any aquarium.

  • Gh: 3-5

Please Note: All fish shipments have a 100% Live Arrival Guarantee when shipping with Overnight (1 Day) option. All other options void our Live Arrival Guarantee for fish. 

Frequently Asked Questions

1. How big do Serpae Tetras get?

Serpae Tetras usually grow to about 1.5 to 2 inches long. Their small size makes them suitable for a variety of tank setups, including smaller community tanks.

2. What tank size is best for Serpae Tetras?

A minimum of 20 gallons is recommended for Serpae Tetras. Since they are schooling fish, they should be kept in groups, and a larger tank allows them to swim and interact comfortably.

3. Are Serpae Tetras peaceful fish?

Serpae Tetras can sometimes be fin nippers, especially if kept in small groups. To reduce this behavior, keep them in a school of at least six to eight fish, and pair them with other fish that aren’t shy or slow-moving.

4. What should I feed Serpae Tetras?

Serpae Tetras are omnivores and do well on a varied diet. High-quality flake food, small pellets, and treats like frozen brine shrimp or bloodworms keep them healthy and help bring out their vibrant colors.

5. Can Serpae Tetras live with other types of fish?

Serpae Tetras can be kept with other active, similarly sized fish, such as other tetras, rasboras, and larger Corydoras. Avoid pairing them with slow-moving or long-finned species, as Serpae Tetras may nip at them, especially in smaller groups.
